votes.reason: The Python code correctly identifies the first string with the maximum length, which exactly matches the Coq specification requiring the result to be strictly longer than any preceding strings and at least as long as all strings in the list.

The Python code correctly identifies the first string with the maximum length, which exactly matches the Coq specification requiring all preceding strings to be strictly shorter and all strings to be at most as long.

The Python code correctly identifies the first string of maximum length, which perfectly matches the Coq specification requiring the result to be the maximum length string with no preceding strings of equal or greater length.

Both specifications find the first string with maximum length. The Coq spec requires: (1) result is in strings, (2) all elements before it are strictly shorter, (3) all elements in strings are at most as long. This means result is the first occurrence of a maximum-length string. The Python code iterates through strings, updating longest only when a strictly longer string is found (len(s) > len(longest)), which also selects the first maximum-length string. Both return None/null for empty lists and the same string for non-empty lists.

Both specifications find the first string with maximum length. The Coq spec requires: (1) result is in strings, (2) all elements before it are strictly shorter, (3) all elements in strings are at most as long. This means result is the first occurrence of a maximum-length string. The Python code iterates through strings, updating longest only when a strictly longer string is found (len(s) > len(longest)), which also finds the first maximum-length string. Both return None/null for empty lists and the same string for non-empty lists.

Both specifications find the first string with maximum length. The Coq spec requires result to be Some s where s appears in strings, all elements before s are strictly shorter, and no element is longer than s - this describes the first occurrence of a maximum-length string. The Python code iterates through strings, updating longest only when a strictly longer string is found (len(s) > len(longest)), which also returns the first maximum-length string. Both return None/null for empty lists and the same string for non-empty lists.

For empty lists it checks result is None; for non-empty lists it selects the first maximum-length element (using > so ties keep the first) and checks result equals that string. This matches the Coq spec under the natural mapping option string ~ None or the string itself.

On empty lists it expects None; otherwise it selects the first maximum-length element and checks result equals it. This matches the Coq spec under the natural encoding of option (None for None, string for Some).

Matches: for empty lists both require result None; for non-empty lists Coq requires result be the first occurrence of a maximum-length string, and Python keeps the earliest max (updates only on strictly greater) and checks result equals it.
